toolResult.textResultForLlm:
1. # CLR Shim Activation Flow Reference
2. 
3. ## Overview
4. 
5. The CLR shim is the .NET Framework's runtime selection and loading layer. It consists of two DLLs:
6. - **mscoree.dll** (the "shell shim") тАФ the public-facing entry point. It is the registered `InprocServer32` for CLR-hosted COM objects, the target of `_CorExeMain` for managed EXE launch, and the DLL that legacy hosting APIs (`CorBindToRuntimeEx`, etc.) are exported from.
7. - **mscoreei.dll** тАФ the actual shim implementation where all the runtime selection logic, logging, activation decisions, and policy evaluation live. mscoree.dll loads and forwards into mscoreei.dll.
8. 
9. The shim supports loading CLR v2.0 and CLR v4.0 side-by-side in the same process (in-proc SxS). Note that .NET 2.0, 3.0, and 3.5 all share CLR v2.0 (v2.0.50727), while .NET 4.0 through 4.8.x all share CLR v4.0 (v4.0.30319).
10. 
11. ## Key Concepts
12. 
13. ### The v4 In-Proc SxS Design
14. 
15. .NET 4.0 added the ability for multiple runtimes (v2.0 and v4.0) to coexist in the same process. This required a complete rethinking of the hosting API surface. A new set of APIs тАФ the **metahost APIs** (defined in metahost.h: `ICLRMetaHost`, `ICLRMetaHostPolicy`, `ICLRRuntimeInfo`) тАФ was introduced with a multi-runtime view of the world. The entire existing mscoree.h API surface became "legacy."
16. 
17. The central design constraint: **installing v4 must be non-impactful** тАФ it must not change the behavior of any existing component already on the machine.
18. 
19. ### Legacy Shim APIs vs. Metahost APIs
20. 
21. **Metahost APIs** (`IsLegacyBind: 0`): The v4+ hosting APIs. These can enumerate all installed runtimes, target specific versions, and support side-by-side loading. This is the normal path for managed EXE launch (`_CorExeMain`) and v4+ COM activation.
22. 
23. **Legacy shim APIs** (`IsLegacyBind: 1`): The entire pre-v4 API surface. These have a **single-runtime-per-process** view of the world, because before v4 there could only be one runtime in a process. The legacy API category encompasses:
24. 
25. 1. **`CorBindToRuntimeEx` and friends** тАФ Most flat exports of mscoree.dll defined in mscoree.h (`GetCORSystemDirectory`, `GetCORVersion`, `LoadLibraryShim`, etc.), plus the strong name APIs from strongname.h
26. 2. **Pre-v4 COM activation** тАФ `CoCreateInstance` of a CLSID whose latest registration is against a pre-v4 runtime version. This includes `new` on such a coclass from managed code, or `Activator.CreateInstance` via `Type.GetTypeFromCLSID`.
27. 3. **Pre-v4 IJW (mixed-mode) activation** тАФ Calling into a native export on a pre-v4 mixed-mode assembly
28. 4. **Native activation of runtime-provided COM CLSIDs** тАФ e.g., `CoCreateInstance` on `ICLRRuntimeHost`'s CLSID
29. 5. **Native activation of managed framework CLSIDs** тАФ e.g., `CoCreateInstance` on `System.ArrayList`'s CLSID (extremely rare)
30. 
31. ### The Legacy Runtime
32. 
33. Because legacy APIs have a single-runtime view, once any legacy codepath chooses a runtime version, that becomes **the** legacy runtime for the process (`g_pLegacyAPIRuntimeInfo`). All subsequent legacy API calls see and use this same runtime for the remainder of the process lifetime. After a version has been chosen by one of these codepaths, that's the version ALL of them see.
34. 
35. If v4.0 is bound as the legacy runtime (through any mechanism тАФ explicit version string, config with `useLegacyV2RuntimeActivationPolicy`, etc.), all subsequent legacy codepaths will use v4.0.
36. 
37. ### Whidbey Capping
38. 
39. All legacy shim API codepaths had roll-forward semantics тАФ they would find and use the latest installed runtime. **Whidbey capping** restricts ("caps") these roll-forward semantics at v2.0 (codename "Whidbey"), meaning **by default, none of the legacy codepaths see v4 at all**. This is what makes v4 installation non-impactful: existing components using legacy APIs continue to behave exactly as they did before v4 was installed.
40. 
41. When `IsCapped: 1` in the logs, the shim will not consider any runtime above v2.0.50727 when enumerating installed runtimes. This has an important and intentional side effect: **on a v4-only machine (no .NET 3.5 installed), legacy codepaths see NO runtimes at all** тАФ the machine appears to have nothing installed from their perspective.
42. 
43. **Capping does NOT prevent loading v4+ if:**
44. - A specific legitimate post-Whidbey version string is explicitly passed to a legacy API (e.g., `CorBindToRuntimeEx("v4.0.30319", ...)`) тАФ the APIs will happily load it
45. - A config file `<supportedRuntime>` explicitly names a v4+ version AND `useLegacyV2RuntimeActivationPolicy="true"` is set
46. - The legacy runtime is already bound to v4+ (all subsequent legacy calls reuse it)
47. 
48. ### useLegacyV2RuntimeActivationPolicy
49. 
50. This config attribute is the primary mechanism for making legacy codepaths see v4. Setting `useLegacyV2RuntimeActivationPolicy="true"` in the `<startup>` element tells the shim to treat all runtimes as candidates for legacy API codepaths. It is **mostly equivalent to calling `CorBindToRuntimeEx` with the full v4 version string**.
51. 
52. It can be used:
53. - With one or more `<supportedRuntime>` entries to direct which runtime is chosen
54. - With other config options

15. .NET 4.0 added the ability for multiple runtimes (v2.0 and v4.0) to coexist in the same process. This required a complete rethinking of the hosting API surface. A new set of APIs тАФ the **metahost APIs** (defined in metahost.h: `ICLRMetaHost`, `ICLRMetaHostPolicy`, `ICLRRuntimeInfo`) тАФ was introduced with a multi-runtime view of the world. The entire existing mscoree.h API surface became "legacy."
16. 
17. The central design constraint: **installing v4 must be non-impactful** тАФ it must not change the behavior of any existing component already on the machine.
18. 
19. ### Legacy Shim APIs vs. Metahost APIs
20. 
21. **Metahost APIs** (`IsLegacyBind: 0`): The v4+ hosting APIs. These can enumerate all installed runtimes, target specific versions, and support side-by-side loading. This is the normal path for managed EXE launch (`_CorExeMain`) and v4+ COM activation.
22. 
23. **Legacy shim APIs** (`IsLegacyBind: 1`): The entire pre-v4 API surface. These have a **single-runtime-per-process** view of the world, because before v4 there could only be one runtime in a process. The legacy API category encompasses:
24. 
25. 1. **`CorBindToRuntimeEx` and friends** тАФ Most flat exports of mscoree.dll defined in mscoree.h (`GetCORSystemDirectory`, `GetCORVersion`, `LoadLibraryShim`, etc.), plus the strong name APIs from strongname.h
26. 2. **Pre-v4 COM activation** тАФ `CoCreateInstance` of a CLSID whose latest registration is against a pre-v4 runtime version. This includes `new` on such a coclass from managed code, or `Activator.CreateInstance` via `Type.GetTypeFromCLSID`.
27. 3. **Pre-v4 IJW (mixed-mode) activation** тАФ Calling into a native export on a pre-v4 mixed-mode assembly
28. 4. **Native activation of runtime-provided COM CLSIDs** тАФ e.g., `CoCreateInstance` on `ICLRRuntimeHost`'s CLSID
29. 5. **Native activation of managed framework CLSIDs** тАФ e.g., `CoCreateInstance` on `System.ArrayList`'s CLSID (extremely rare)
30. 
31. ### The Legacy Runtime
32. 
33. Because legacy APIs have a single-runtime view, once any legacy codepath chooses a runtime version, that becomes **the** legacy runtime for the process (`g_pLegacyAPIRuntimeInfo`). All subsequent legacy API calls see and use this same runtime for the remainder of the process lifetime. After a version has been chosen by one of these codepaths, that's the version ALL of them see.
34. 
35. If v4.0 is bound as the legacy runtime (through any mechanism тАФ explicit version string, config with `useLegacyV2RuntimeActivationPolicy`, etc.), all subsequent legacy codepaths will use v4.0.
36. 
37. ### Whidbey Capping
38. 
39. All legacy shim API codepaths had roll-forward semantics тАФ they would find and use the latest installed runtime. **Whidbey capping** restricts ("caps") these roll-forward semantics at v2.0 (codename "Whidbey"), meaning **by default, none of the legacy codepaths see v4 at all**. This is what makes v4 installation non-impactful: existing components using legacy APIs continue to behave exactly as they did before v4 was installed.
40. 
41. When `IsCapped: 1` in the logs, the shim will not consider any runtime above v2.0.50727 when enumerating installed runtimes. This has an important and intentional side effect: **on a v4-only machine (no .NET 3.5 installed), legacy codepaths see NO runtimes at all** тАФ the machine appears to have nothing installed from their perspective.
42. 
43. **Capping does NOT prevent loading v4+ if:**
44. - A specific legitimate post-Whidbey version string is explicitly passed to a legacy API (e.g., `CorBindToRuntimeEx("v4.0.30319", ...)`) тАФ the APIs will happily load it
45. - A config file `<supportedRuntime>` explicitly names a v4+ version AND `useLegacyV2RuntimeActivationPolicy="true"` is set
46. - The legacy runtime is already bound to v4+ (all subsequent legacy calls reuse it)
47. 
48. ### useLegacyV2RuntimeActivationPolicy
49. 
50. This config attribute is the primary mechanism for making legacy codepaths see v4. Setting `useLegacyV2RuntimeActivationPolicy="true"` in the `<startup>` element tells the shim to treat all runtimes as candidates for legacy API codepaths. It is **mostly equivalent to calling `CorBindToRuntimeEx` with the full v4 version string**.
51. 
52. It can be used:
53. - With one or more `<supportedRuntime>` entries to direct which runtime is chosen
54. - With other config options
55. - With **no `<supportedRuntime>` entries at all** тАФ in which case legacy APIs can simply enumerate and discover v4
56. 
57. ```xml
58. <!-- Common usage: direct legacy codepaths to v4.0 -->
59. <configuration>
60.   <startup useLegacyV2RuntimeActivationPolicy="true">
61.     <supportedRuntime version="v4.0"/>
62.   </startup>
63. </configuration>
64. ```
65. 
66. ```xml
67. <!-- Also valid: just let legacy APIs enumerate v4 -->
68. <configuration>
69.   <startup useLegacyV2RuntimeActivationPolicy="true">
70.   </startup>
71. </configuration>
72. ```
73. 
74. **Side effect:** Enabling this attribute turns off in-proc SxS with pre-v4 runtimes тАФ it locks them out of the process. The legacy runtime becomes v4, and pre-v4 runtimes cannot load.
75. 
76. **Common reasons to use it:**
77. - Loading pre-v4 mixed-mode (IJW) assemblies into v4
78. - Making legacy API calls (P/Invoke to `GetCORSystemDirectory`, etc.) return v4 paths
79. - Ensuring COM activation of managed objects uses the current runtime (v4) instead of falling back to v2
80. 
81. **Why it isn't the default:** If it were the default, installing v4 would be impactful тАФ it would change behavior of existing components, violating the core design constraint.
82. 
83. ### Feature-on-Demand (FOD)
84. 
85. When the shim fails to find a runtime, it can trigger installation of .NET 3.5 via Windows Feature-on-Demand. The FOD path:
86. 
87. 1. Only activates on Windows 8+ (not on ARM, not in AppX)
88. 2. Re-runs version computation to check if v2.0.50727 would resolve the request
89. 3. If yes, and if the `SEM_FAILCRITICALERRORS` process error mode flag is NOT set, launches `fondue.exe /enable-feature:NetFx3`
90. 4. If `SEM_FAILCRITICALERRORS` IS set, logs "Could have launched..." but does not show the dialog
91. 5. FOD only fires once per process (`g_FeatureOnDemandHasBeenLaunched` flag)
92. 
93. FOD only supports installing .NET 3.5 (v2.0.50727). It cannot install v4.0+.
94. 
95. > тЪая╕П **Recent Windows versions:** Starting with Windows 11 Insider Preview Build 27965 and future platform releases, .NET 3.5 is **no longer available as a Windows optional component**. It must be installed from a standalone MSI. On these systems, the FOD dialog (`fondue.exe /enable-feature:NetFx3`) will not succeed even if it fires. This change does not affect Windows 10 or Windows 11 through 25H2. .NET Framework 3.5 reaches end of support on January 9, 2029.
96. 
97. ### SEM_FAILCRITICALERRORS Inheritance
98. 
99. The `SEM_FAILCRITICALERRORS` flag is part of the process error mode, set via `SetErrorMode()`. It is **inherited by child processes**. This means:
100. 
101. - If a build system or script calls `SetErrorMode(SEM_FAILCRITICALERRORS)`, all processes it spawns will have the flag set
102. - If you launch a process directly from a clean cmd.exe, the flag is typically 0
103. - This is the most common reason FOD behavior changes between different launch methods
104. 
105. ## The Version Resolution Decision Tree
106. 
107. This is the order in which `ComputeVersionString` resolves a runtime version:
108. 
109. ```
110. 1. COMPLUS_OnlyUseLatestCLR=1? (internal testing only тАФ not supported)
111.    тФФтФА Yes тЖТ FindLatestVersion (uncapped) тЖТ done
112. 
113. 2. Config file has <supportedRuntime> entries?
114.    тФФтФА Yes тЖТ For each entry (in order):
115.       тФЬтФА Is this version installed? Check SKU compatibility.
116.       тФЬтФА If IsCapped AND UseLegacyV2RuntimeActivationPolicy=0:
117.       тФВ   тФФтФА Only consider v2.0.x entries (skip v4.0+)
118.       тФЬтФА If IsCapped AND UseLegacyV2RuntimeActivationPolicy=1:
119.       тФВ   тФФтФА Consider ALL entries including v4.0+ (cap is lifted;
120.       тФВ      chosen runtime becomes the legacy runtime)
121.       тФФтФА First installed match wins тЖТ done
122. 
123. 2b. UseLegacyV2RuntimeActivationPolicy=1 but NO <supportedRuntime> entries?
124.    тФФтФА Legacy APIs can enumerate all installed runtimes (cap lifted)
125.       тЖТ Falls through to FindLatestVersion (uncapped)
126. 
127. 3. Config file has <requiredRuntime> element? (legacy v1.0/v1.1)
128.    тФФтФА Yes тЖТ Use that version тЖТ done
129. 
130. 4. COMPLUS_Version environment variable set?
131.    тФФтФА Yes тЖТ Use that version тЖТ done
132. 
133. 5. Host provided a default version?
134.    тФФтФА Yes тЖТ Use that version тЖТ done
135. 
136. 6. Legacy runtime already bound (g_pLegacyAPIRuntimeInfo != NULL)?
137.    тФФтФА Yes тЖТ Use that version тЖТ done
138. 
139. 7. Binary has a PE header version (managed assemblies only)?
140.    тФФтФА Yes тЖТ Use that version тЖТ done
141. 
142. 8. None of the above?
143.    тФФтФА FindLatestVersion (respects capping)
144.       тФЬтФА If capped: enumerate installed runtimes тЙдv2.0.50727
145.       тФЬтФА If uncapped: enumerate all installed runtimes
146.       тФФтФА Return highest match, or (null) if none found
147. ```
148. 
149. If resolution returns `(null)` тЖТ runtime not found тЖТ enter error/FOD path.
150. 
151. ## Entry Point Details
152. 
153. ### _CorExeMain (Managed EXE Launch)
154. 
155. 1. OS loader recognizes .NET PE header, calls `_CorExeMain`
156. 2. Shim reads PE header for built-with version
157. 3. Looks for `{exe}.config` for `<supportedRuntime>` entries
158. 4. `IsLegacyBind: 0`, `IsCapped: 0` (normal modern bind)
159. 5. Follows decision tree above
160. 6. Loads runtime, transfers control to managed entry point
161. 
162. ### DllGetClassObject (COM Activation)
163. 
164. This is the most complex path and the most common source of activation issues.
165. 
166. 1. Something calls `CoCreateInstance` for a CLSID registered under mscoree.dll
167. 2. Shim looks up the CLSID in the registry:
168.    - `HKCR\CLSID\{guid}\InprocServer32` тЖТ checks if `(Default)` is mscoree.dll
169.    - Enumerates version subkeys (e.g., `2.0.50727`, `4.0.30319`)
170.    - Reads `RuntimeVersion`, `Assembly`, `Class`, `ImplementedInThisVersion` from subkeys
171. 3. Determines if this is a legacy or modern COM object:
172.    - If CLSID has only old version subkeys or is a known framework COM object тЖТ **legacy path**
173.    - Legacy path sets `IsLegacyBind: 1`, `IsCapped: 1`
174. 4. Version resolution follows the decision tree
175. 5. If the legacy runtime is already bound, reuses it (skips the entire search)
176. 
177. **Critical implication:** For native EXEs doing COM activation, if no config file exists and the legacy runtime is not already bound, a capped legacy bind will enumerate only тЙдv2.0 runtimes. On a machine without .NET 3.5, this finds nothing and triggers the error/FOD path.
178. 
179. ### CorBindToRuntimeEx (Legacy Hosting)
180. 
181. 1. Caller specifies a version (or NULL for default)
182. 2. If version is NULL, uses `FindLatestVersion` (respects capping)
183. 3. If version specified, attempts to load that exact version
184. 4. On success, sets `g_pLegacyAPIRuntimeInfo` (the process-global legacy runtime)
185. 5. Subsequent calls must match the same runtime or fail with `CLR_E_SHIM_LEGACYRUNTIMEALREADYBOUND`
186. 
187. ### LoadLibraryShim (Legacy DLL Loading)
188. 
189. 1. Loads a framework DLL by name (e.g., `diasymreader.dll`)
190. 2. If version specified, loads from that runtime's directory
191. 3. If no version, uses legacy runtime if bound, otherwise finds latest
192. 
193. ## Config File Resolution
194. 
195. The shim looks for config files at:
196. 
197. 1. **Activation config**: Set via `COMPLUS_ApplicationMigrationRuntimeActivationConfigPath` (rare)
198. 2. **Host config**: Provided by hosting API caller (rare)
199. 3. **App config**: `{exe_path}.config` (most common)
200. 
201. ### Config for legacy codepaths
202. 
203. To make legacy shim API codepaths (including capped COM activation) see v4.0:
204. 
205. ```xml
206. <?xml version="1.0"?>
207. <configuration>
208.   <startup useLegacyV2RuntimeActivationPolicy="true">
209.     <supportedRuntime version="v4.0"/>
210.   </startup>
211. </configuration>
212. ```
213. 
214. **`useLegacyV2RuntimeActivationPolicy="true"` is the key piece** тАФ it tells the shim to treat all runtimes as candidates for legacy codepaths, lifting the Whidbey cap. The `<supportedRuntime>` element directs which version is chosen, but even without it, the attribute alone allows legacy APIs to enumerate v4.
215. 
216. Without `useLegacyV2RuntimeActivationPolicy`, rolling forward to v4 via `<supportedRuntime>` works fine for the primary EXE load (which uses the metahost path), but legacy codepaths (COM activation, P/Invoke to mscoree.h APIs, etc.) remain capped and will still look for v2.0.
217. 
218. **Trade-off:** Setting `useLegacyV2RuntimeActivationPolicy="true"` turns off in-proc SxS with pre-v4 runtimes. This is usually acceptable for build tools and utilities, but should be considered for applications that need to host both v2 and v4.
219. 
220. ## Environment Variables That Affect Activation
221. 
222. | Variable | Effect |
223. |----------|--------|
224. | `COMPLUS_CLRLoadLogDir` | Directory for activation logs (**must already exist**; no error if missing) |
225. | `COMPLUS_Version` | Force a specific runtime version |
226. | `COMPLUS_DefaultVersion` | Default version for FindLatestVersion |
227. | `COMPLUS_Fod` | If 0, disable FOD entirely |
228. | `COMPLUS_FodConservativeMode` | If 1, only log FOD commands (don't execute) when error dialogs suppressed |
229. | `COMPLUS_ErrorDialog` | Override SEM_FAILCRITICALERRORS for error dialog display |
230. | `COMPLUS_ApplicationMigrationRuntimeActivationConfigPath` | Override config file path |
231. | `COMPLUS_OnlyUseLatestCLR` | тЪая╕П **Internal testing only тАФ not supported.** If 1, ignores capping and uses latest installed runtime. Do not use in production. |
232.

 5. If the legacy runtime is already bound, reuses it (skips the entire search)
 
 **Critical implication:** For native EXEs doing COM activation, if no config file exists and the legacy runtime is not already bound, a capped legacy bind will enumerate only тЙдv2.0 runtimes. On a machine without .NET 3.5, this finds nothing and triggers the error/FOD path.
 
 ### CorBindToRuntimeEx (Legacy Hosting)
 
 1. Caller specifies a version (or NULL for default)
 2. If version is NULL, uses `FindLatestVersion` (respects capping)
 3. If version specified, attempts to load that exact version
 4. On success, sets `g_pLegacyAPIRuntimeInfo` (the process-global legacy runtime)
 5. Subsequent calls must match the same runtime or fail with `CLR_E_SHIM_LEGACYRUNTIMEALREADYBOUND`
 
 ### LoadLibraryShim (Legacy DLL Loading)
 
 1. Loads a framework DLL by name (e.g., `diasymreader.dll`)
 2. If version specified, loads from that runtime's directory
 3. If no version, uses legacy runtime if bound, otherwise finds latest
 
 ## Config File Resolution
 
 The shim looks for config files at:
 
 1. **Activation config**: Set via `COMPLUS_ApplicationMigrationRuntimeActivationConfigPath` (rare)
 2. **Host config**: Provided by hosting API caller (rare)
 3. **App config**: `{exe_path}.config` (most common)
 
 ### Config for legacy codepaths
 
 To make legacy shim API codepaths (including capped COM activation) see v4.0:
 
 ```xml
 <?xml version="1.0"?>
 <configuration>
   <startup useLegacyV2RuntimeActivationPolicy="true">
     <supportedRuntime version="v4.0"/>
   </startup>
 </configuration>
 ```
 
 **`useLegacyV2RuntimeActivationPolicy="true"` is the key piece** тАФ it tells the shim to treat all runtimes as candidates for legacy codepaths, lifting the Whidbey cap. The `<supportedRuntime>` element directs which version is chosen, but even without it, the attribute alone allows legacy APIs to enumerate v4.

 |----------|--------|
 | `COMPLUS_CLRLoadLogDir` | Directory for activation logs (**must already exist**; no error if missing) |
 | `COMPLUS_Version` | Force a specific runtime version |
 | `COMPLUS_DefaultVersion` | Default version for FindLatestVersion |
 | `COMPLUS_Fod` | If 0, disable FOD entirely |
 | `COMPLUS_FodConservativeMode` | If 1, only log FOD commands (don't execute) when error dialogs suppressed |
 | `COMPLUS_ErrorDialog` | Override SEM_FAILCRITICALERRORS for error dialog display |
 | `COMPLUS_ApplicationMigrationRuntimeActivationConfigPath` | Override config file path |
 | `COMPLUS_OnlyUseLatestCLR` | тЪая╕П **Internal testing only тАФ not supported.** If 1, ignores capping and uses latest installed runtime. Do not use in production. |

28.     тФВ   тФФтФАтФА ImplementedInThisVersion = ""       (native CLR component, not interop)
29.     тФВ
30.     тФФтФАтФА 4.0.30319                    (version subkey тАФ CLR v4 registration)
31.         тФВ   (Default) = "4.0.30319"
32.         тФВ   Assembly = "FullAssemblyName"
33.         тФВ   Class = "Namespace.ClassName"
34.         тФВ   RuntimeVersion = "v4.0.30319"
35.         тФФтФАтФА ImplementedInThisVersion = ""
36. ```
37. 
38. ### Key Registry Values
39. 
40. **`(Default)` under InprocServer32**: Must be `mscoree.dll` (or full path) for the shim to handle it.
41. 
42. **Version subkeys** (e.g., `2.0.50727`, `4.0.30319`): Each subkey represents a CLR version that registered this CLSID. The shim uses these to determine which runtime version(s) can host the object.
43. 
44. **`RuntimeVersion`**: The CLR version to load for this object. Read from the highest applicable version subkey.
45. 
46. **`Assembly` and `Class`**: For managed COM interop тАФ the managed assembly and type that implement the COM object. If present, the CLR loads this assembly and creates the managed type.
47. 
48. **`ImplementedInThisVersion`**: When this value exists (even if empty), it marks the COM class as a **native CLR component** тАФ something implemented inside the runtime itself (like the metadata APIs, symbol readers, etc.), not a managed interop object. The shim handles these differently from managed COM.
49. 
50. **`SupportedRuntimeVersions`** (directly under InprocServer32): Semicolon-delimited list of runtime versions. Acts like a `<supportedRuntime>` list in a config file. Example: `v2.0.50727;v4.0.30319`.
51. 
52. **`MasterCLSID`**: If present on the CLSID key, redirects to another CLSID for version lookup purposes. Used for COM objects that have been superseded.
53. 
54. ## The DllGetClassObject Decision Flow
55. 
56. ```
57. DllGetClassObject(rclsid, riid, ppv)
58. тФВ
59. тФЬтФА 1. Is this an AppX process with a disallowed CLSID? тЖТ E_NOTIMPL
60. тФВ
61. тФЬтФА 2. Is this CLSID cached in g_pCVMList?
62. тФВ     тФФтФА Yes тЖТ Call cached DllGetClassObject directly тЖТ done
63. тФВ
64. тФЬтФА 3. Try LoadUnmanagedCOMObject() тАФ handles non-CLR objects
65. тФВ     тФФтФА Success тЖТ done (no CLR involved)
66. тФВ
67. тФЬтФА 4. Classify the CLSID:
68. тФВ     тФЬтФА IsClrHostedLegacyComObject()? тЖТ Legacy COM object
69. тФВ     тФЬтФА IsCLSIDImplementedInFrameworkAssembly()? тЖТ Framework assembly COM
70. тФВ     тФФтФА Neither тЖТ Modern managed COM
71. тФВ
72. тФЬтФА 5. Determine activation path:
73. тФВ     тФВ
74. тФВ     тФЬтФА MODERN PATH (not legacy, not framework):
75. тФВ     тФВ   тФФтФА GetClassObjectForManagedType()
76. тФВ     тФВ       тФЬтФА Uses ICLRMetaHostPolicy with METAHOST_POLICY_HIGHCOMPAT
77. тФВ     тФВ       тФЬтФА IsLegacyBind: 0, IsCapped: 0
78. тФВ     тФВ       тФФтФА Config file + version subkeys guide runtime selection
79. тФВ     тФВ
80. тФВ     тФФтФА LEGACY PATH (legacy or framework COM):
81. тФВ         тФФтФА RequestRuntimeDll()
82. тФВ             тФЬтФА Sets IsLegacyBind: 1, IsCapped: 1
83. тФВ             тФЬтФА Sets fLatestVersion: TRUE (find latest within cap)
84. тФВ             тФЬтФА If g_pLegacyAPIRuntimeInfo already set тЖТ use it directly
85. тФВ             тФФтФА Otherwise тЖТ ComputeVersionString тЖТ FindLatestVersion (capped)
86. тФВ
87. тФФтФА 6. Load the CLR, get class factory, return object
88. ```
89. 
90. ## Why Native Build Tools Trigger COM Activation
91. 
92. Native tools like `link.exe`, `mt.exe`, `CL.exe` are not .NET applications, but they may use COM objects that are implemented inside the CLR:
93. 
94. - **Diasymreader** (`{E5CB7A31-7512-11D2-89CE-0080C792E5D8}`): Debug symbol writer/reader, used by tools that produce or consume PDB files
95. - **SymBinder** (`{0A29FF9E-7F9C-4437-8B11-F424491E3931}`): Symbol binder for debug information
96. - **ALink** (`{B79B0ACD-F5CD-409B-B5A5-A16244610B92}`): Assembly linker
97. 
98. These COM objects have `InprocServer32 = mscoree.dll` and go through the full shim activation path. Because they're activated via `DllGetClassObject` (not via a managed EXE launch), they take the **legacy COM path** with `IsCapped: 1`.
99. 
100. ## The Legacy Runtime Binding Order Problem
101. 
102. The order of activations within a process determines behavior:
103. 
104. ### Scenario A: Primary load first, COM activation second (common for link.exe, CL.exe)
105. 
106. ```
107. 1. ClrCreateInstance тЖТ ICLRMetaHostPolicy::GetRequestedRuntime
108.    тЖТ Config says supportedRuntime v4.0 тЖТ Loads v4.0 тЖТ Success
109.    тЖТ g_pLegacyAPIRuntimeInfo may or may not be set yet
110. 
111. 2. DllGetClassObject for diasymreader
112.    тЖТ Legacy bind, IsCapped: 1
113.    тЖТ If g_pLegacyAPIRuntimeInfo is set to v4.0 тЖТ uses v4.0 тЖТ OK
114.    тЖТ If NOT set тЖТ FindLatestVersion (capped to v2.0) тЖТ fails on machines without 3.5
115. ```
116. 
117. ### Scenario B: COM activation is the ONLY activation (common for mt.exe)
118. 
119. ```
120. 1. DllGetClassObject for diasymreader
121.    тЖТ Legacy bind, IsCapped: 1
122.    тЖТ No legacy runtime bound yet
123.    тЖТ No config file
124.    тЖТ FindLatestVersion (capped to v2.0) тЖТ (null) тЖТ ERROR тЖТ FOD
125. ```
126. 
127. ### Scenario C: Legacy hosting API sets the runtime first
128. 
129. ```
130. 1. CorBindToRuntimeEx(v4.0) тЖТ binds g_pLegacyAPIRuntimeInfo to v4.0
131. 2. DllGetClassObject for anything тЖТ legacy bind тЖТ uses g_pLegacyAPIRuntimeInfo (v4.0) тЖТ OK
132. ```
133. 
134. ## Diagnosing CLSID Issues
135. 
136. ### Check the CLSID registration
137. 
138. ```powershell
139. $clsid = '{E5CB7A31-7512-11D2-89CE-0080C792E5D8}'
140. 
141. # Main CLSID entry
142. Get-ItemProperty "Registry::HKCR\CLSID\$clsid" -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue
143. 
144. # InprocServer32 (should be mscoree.dll)
145. Get-ItemProperty "Registry::HKCR\CLSID\$clsid\InprocServer32" -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue
146. 
147. # Version subkeys тАФ what runtimes registered this CLSID?
148. Get-ChildItem "Registry::HKCR\CLSID\$clsid\InprocServer32" -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue |
149.     ForEach-Object {
150.         Write-Output "`n--- $($_.PSChildName) ---"
151.         Get-ItemProperty "Registry::$($_.Name)" -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue
152.     }
153. ```
154. 
155. ### What to look for
156. 
157. | Observation | Meaning |
158. |-------------|---------|
159. | Only a `4.0.30319` subkey exists, no `2.0.50727` | .NET 3.5 was never installed (no v2 registration). Capped legacy binds will fail. |
160. | Both `2.0.50727` and `4.0.30319` subkeys exist | Both runtimes have registered this CLSID. Capped binds can use v2, uncapped can use v4. |
161. | `ImplementedInThisVersion` is present | This is a native CLR component (not managed interop). |
162. | `Assembly` and `Class` are present | This is a managed COM interop registration. |
163. | `SupportedRuntimeVersions` exists | Acts as an inline supportedRuntime list (semicolon-delimited). |
164. | `InprocServer32\(Default)` is NOT mscoree.dll | This CLSID is not CLR-hosted тАФ shim is not involved. |
165. 
166. ### Registration corruption from build races
167. 
168. If a build process registers/unregisters COM objects (e.g., via `regasm.exe`), concurrent registrations can corrupt CLSID entries:
169. 
170. - A version subkey may be deleted or partially written
171. - `Assembly`/`Class` values may be missing or point to the wrong type
172. - `RuntimeVersion` may be incorrect
173. 
174. Check for partial registrations by verifying that all expected values exist under each version subkey.

 # COM Activation Through the CLR Shim тАФ Reference
 
 ## How COM Objects End Up in mscoree.dll
 
 When a COM object is implemented in managed code (or is a CLR-internal component like diasymreader), its `InprocServer32` registry entry points to `mscoree.dll`. When `CoCreateInstance` is called for such a CLSID, the OS loads mscoree.dll and calls its `DllGetClassObject`.
 
 The shim then must determine:
 1. Which CLR version should host this COM object
 2. Whether the CLR is already loaded in this process
 3. Whether to use legacy or modern activation
 
 ## CLSID Registry Layout
 
 ```
 HKCR\CLSID\{guid}
 тФВ   (Default) = "Friendly Name"
 тФВ   MasterCLSID = "{other-guid}"    (optional, for redirected CLSIDs)
 тФВ
 тФФтФАтФА InprocServer32
     тФВ   (Default) = "C:\Windows\System32\mscoree.dll"
     тФВ   ThreadingModel = "Both"
     тФВ
     тФЬтФАтФА 2.0.50727                    (version subkey тАФ CLR v2 registration)
     тФВ   тФВ   (Default) = "2.0.50727"
     тФВ   тФВ   Assembly = "FullAssemblyName"       (managed interop)
     тФВ   тФВ   Class = "Namespace.ClassName"       (managed interop)
     тФВ   тФВ   RuntimeVersion = "v2.0.50727"       (which CLR to load)
     тФВ   тФФтФАтФА ImplementedInThisVersion = ""       (native CLR component, not interop)
     тФВ
     тФФтФАтФА 4.0.30319                    (version subkey тАФ CLR v4 registration)
         тФВ   (Default) = "4.0.30319"
         тФВ   Assembly = "FullAssemblyName"
         тФВ   Class = "Namespace.ClassName"
         тФВ   RuntimeVersion = "v4.0.30319"
         тФФтФАтФА ImplementedInThisVersion = ""
 ```
 
 ### Key Registry Values
 
 **`(Default)` under InprocServer32**: Must be `mscoree.dll` (or full path) for the shim to handle it.
 
 **Version subkeys** (e.g., `2.0.50727`, `4.0.30319`): Each subkey represents a CLR version that registered this CLSID. The shim uses these to determine which runtime version(s) can host the object.
 
 **`RuntimeVersion`**: The CLR version to load for this object. Read from the highest applicable version subkey.
 
 **`Assembly` and `Class`**: For managed COM interop тАФ the managed assembly and type that implement the COM object. If present, the CLR loads this assembly and creates the managed type.
 
 **`ImplementedInThisVersion`**: When this value exists (even if empty), it marks the COM class as a **native CLR component** тАФ something implemented inside the runtime itself (like the metadata APIs, symbol readers, etc.), not a managed interop object. The shim handles these differently from managed COM.
 
 **`SupportedRuntimeVersions`** (directly under InprocServer32): Semicolon-delimited list of runtime versions. Acts like a `<supportedRuntime>` list in a config file. Example: `v2.0.50727;v4.0.30319`.
 
 **`MasterCLSID`**: If present on the CLSID key, redirects to another CLSID for version lookup purposes. Used for COM objects that have been superseded.
 
 ## The DllGetClassObject Decision Flow
 
 ```
 DllGetClassObject(rclsid, riid, ppv)
 тФВ
 тФЬтФА 1. Is this an AppX process with a disallowed CLSID? тЖТ E_NOTIMPL
 тФВ
 тФЬтФА 2. Is this CLSID cached in g_pCVMList?
 тФВ     тФФтФА Yes тЖТ Call cached DllGetClassObject directly тЖТ done
 тФВ
 тФЬтФА 3. Try LoadUnmanagedCOMObject() тАФ handles non-CLR objects
 тФВ     тФФтФА Success тЖТ done (no CLR involved)
 тФВ
 тФЬтФА 4. Classify the CLSID:
 тФВ     тФЬтФА IsClrHostedLegacyComObject()? тЖТ Legacy COM object
 тФВ     тФЬтФА IsCLSIDImplementedInFrameworkAssembly()? тЖТ Framework assembly COM
 тФВ     тФФтФА Neither тЖТ Modern managed COM
 тФВ
 тФЬтФА 5. Determine activation path:
 тФВ     тФВ
 тФВ     тФЬтФА MODERN PATH (not legacy, not framework):
 тФВ     тФВ   тФФтФА GetClassObjectForManagedType()
 тФВ     тФВ       тФЬтФА Uses ICLRMetaHostPolicy with METAHOST_POLICY_HIGHCOMPAT
 тФВ     тФВ       тФЬтФА IsLegacyBind: 0, IsCapped: 0
 тФВ     тФВ       тФФтФА Config file + version subkeys guide runtime selection
 тФВ     тФВ
 тФВ     тФФтФА LEGACY PATH (legacy or framework COM):
 тФВ         тФФтФА RequestRuntimeDll()
 тФВ             тФЬтФА Sets IsLegacyBind: 1, IsCapped: 1
 тФВ             тФЬтФА Sets fLatestVersion: TRUE (find latest within cap)
 тФВ             тФЬтФА If g_pLegacyAPIRuntimeInfo already set тЖТ use it directly
 тФВ             тФФтФА Otherwise тЖТ ComputeVersionString тЖТ FindLatestVersion (capped)
 тФВ
 тФФтФА 6. Load the CLR, get class factory, return object
 ```
 
 ## Why Native Build Tools Trigger COM Activation
 
 Native tools like `link.exe`, `mt.exe`, `CL.exe` are not .NET applications, but they may use COM objects that are implemented inside the CLR:
 
 - **Diasymreader** (`{E5CB7A31-7512-11D2-89CE-0080C792E5D8}`): Debug symbol writer/reader, used by tools that produce or consume PDB files
 - **SymBinder** (`{0A29FF9E-7F9C-4437-8B11-F424491E3931}`): Symbol binder for debug information
 - **ALink** (`{B79B0ACD-F5CD-409B-B5A5-A16244610B92}`): Assembly linker
 
 These COM objects have `InprocServer32 = mscoree.dll` and go through the full shim activation path. Because they're activated via `DllGetClassObject` (not via a managed EXE launch), they take the **legacy COM path** with `IsCapped: 1`.
 
 ## The Legacy Runtime Binding Order Problem
 
 The order of activations within a process determines behavior:
 
 ### Scenario A: Primary load first, COM activation second (common for link.exe, CL.exe)
 
 ```
 1. ClrCreateInstance тЖТ ICLRMetaHostPolicy::GetRequestedRuntime
    тЖТ Config says supportedRuntime v4.0 тЖТ Loads v4.0 тЖТ Success
    тЖТ g_pLegacyAPIRuntimeInfo may or may not be set yet
 
 2. DllGetClassObject for diasymreader
    тЖТ Legacy bind, IsCapped: 1
    тЖТ If g_pLegacyAPIRuntimeInfo is set to v4.0 тЖТ uses v4.0 тЖТ OK
    тЖТ If NOT set тЖТ FindLatestVersion (capped to v2.0) тЖТ fails on machines without 3.5
 ```
 
 ### Scenario B: COM activation is the ONLY activation (common for mt.exe)
 
 ```
 1. DllGetClassObject for diasymreader
    тЖТ Legacy bind, IsCapped: 1
    тЖТ No legacy runtime bound yet
    тЖТ No config file
    тЖТ FindLatestVersion (capped to v2.0) тЖТ (null) тЖТ ERROR тЖТ FOD
 ```
 
 ### Scenario C: Legacy hosting API sets the runtime first
 
 ```
 1. CorBindToRuntimeEx(v4.0) тЖТ binds g_pLegacyAPIRuntimeInfo to v4.0
 2. DllGetClassObject for anything тЖТ legacy bind тЖТ uses g_pLegacyAPIRuntimeInfo (v4.0) тЖТ OK
 ```
 
 ## Diagnosing CLSID Issues
 
 ### Check the CLSID registration
 
 ```powershell
 $clsid = '{E5CB7A31-7512-11D2-89CE-0080C792E5D8}'
 
 # Main CLSID entry
 Get-ItemProperty "Registry::HKCR\CLSID\$clsid" -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue
 
 # InprocServer32 (should be mscoree.dll)
 Get-ItemProperty "Registry::HKCR\CLSID\$clsid\InprocServer32" -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue
 
 # Version subkeys тАФ what runtimes registered this CLSID?
 Get-ChildItem "Registry::HKCR\CLSID\$clsid\InprocServer32" -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue |
     ForEach-Object {
         Write-Output "`n--- $($_.PSChildName) ---"
         Get-ItemProperty "Registry::$($_.Name)" -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue
     }
 ```
 
 ### What to look for
 
 | Observation | Meaning |
 |-------------|---------|
 | Only a `4.0.30319` subkey exists, no `2.0.50727` | .NET 3.5 was never installed (no v2 registration). Capped legacy binds will fail. |
 | Both `2.0.50727` and `4.0.30319` subkeys exist | Both runtimes have registered this CLSID. Capped binds can use v2, uncapped can use v4. |
 | `ImplementedInThisVersion` is present | This is a native CLR component (not managed interop). |
 | `Assembly` and `Class` are present | This is a managed COM interop registration. |
 | `SupportedRuntimeVersions` exists | Acts as an inline supportedRuntime list (semicolon-delimited). |
 | `InprocServer32\(Default)` is NOT mscoree.dll | This CLSID is not CLR-hosted тАФ shim is not involved. |
 
 ### Registration corruption from build races
 
 If a build process registers/unregisters COM objects (e.g., via `regasm.exe`), concurrent registrations can corrupt CLSID entries:
 
 - A version subkey may be deleted or partially written
 - `Assembly`/`Class` values may be missing or point to the wrong type
 - `RuntimeVersion` may be incorrect
 
 Check for partial registrations by verifying that all expected values exist under each version subkey.

1. # CLR Activation Log Format Reference
2. 
3. ## Enabling Logging
4. 
5. Set one of:
6. - **Environment variable:** `COMPLUS_CLRLoadLogDir=C:\path\to\logdir`
7. - **Registry:** `HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\.NETFramework\CLRLoadLogDir` (REG_SZ)
8. - On 64-bit, also `HKLM\SOFTWARE\Wow6432Node\Microsoft\.NETFramework\CLRLoadLogDir` for 32-bit processes
9. 
10. > тЪая╕П **The log directory must already exist.** The shim will not create it. If it doesn't exist, no logs will be written and there will be no error or indication of failure.
11. 
12. Logs are written by mscoreei.dll (the shim implementation DLL loaded by mscoree.dll). They are written while the process runs and **cannot be opened until the process exits**.
13. 
14. ## File Naming
15. 
16. ```
17. {ProcessName}.CLRLoad{NN}.log
18. ```
19. 
20. - `{ProcessName}` = the EXE name (e.g., `mt.exe`, `csc.exe`, `MSBuild.exe`)
21. - `{NN}` = sequence number 00тАУ99 (increments if the previous file exists, one per process instance)
22. - Multiple log files for the same EXE indicate multiple process invocations
23. 
24. ## Line Format
25. 
26. Every line in the log follows this format:
27. 
28. ```
29. ThreadID,TickCount.Milliseconds,Message
30. ```
31. 
32. - **ThreadID**: OS thread ID (decimal) that produced the log entry
33. - **TickCount.Milliseconds**: Time in `GetTickCount()` units тАФ seconds since system boot, with millisecond precision. Useful for ordering events and measuring elapsed time within and across logs.
34. - **Message**: The log message (see below)
35. 
36. ### Header Lines
37. 
38. The first three lines of every log are:
39. 
40. ```
41. {tid},{tick},CLR Loading log for {full_path_to_exe}
42. {tid},{tick},Log started at {time} on {date}
43. {tid},{tick},-----------------------------------
44. ```
45. 
46. The process path in the first line identifies exactly which binary triggered CLR activation.
47. 
48. ## Log Message Reference
49. 
50. ### Entry Points
51. 
52. | Message | Meaning |
53. |---------|---------|
54. | `FunctionCall: _CorExeMain` | Managed EXE launch тАФ the OS loader recognized a .NET assembly |
55. | `FunctionCall: DllGetClassObject. Clsid: {guid}, Iid: {iid}` | COM activation тАФ CoCreateInstance routed through mscoree.dll |
56. | `FunctionCall: ClrCreateInstance, Clsid: {guid}, Iid: {iid}` | Modern v4+ hosting API entry |
57. | `LegacyFunctionCall: CorBindToRuntimeEx. Version: {ver}, BuildFlavor: {flavor}, Flags: {hex}` | Legacy v1/v2 hosting API binding |
58. | `LegacyFunctionCall: LoadLibraryShim. DllName: {dll}, Version: {ver}` | Legacy API to load a framework DLL |
59. | `LegacyFunctionCall: GetFileVersion. Filename: {path}` | Shim reading PE version from a binary |
60. | `MethodCall: ICLRMetaHostPolicy::GetRequestedRuntime. Version: {ver}, Metahost Policy Flags: {hex}, Binary: {path}` | Policy-based runtime request |
61. | `MethodCall: ICLRRuntimeInfo::GetInterface. Clsid: {guid}, Iid: {iid}` | Requesting an interface from a loaded runtime |
62. 
63. ### Version Computation
64. 
65. | Message | Meaning |
66. |---------|---------|
67. | `Input values for ComputeVersionString follow this line` | Start of a version resolution block |
68. | `IsLegacyBind is: {0\|1}` | Whether this is a legacy (pre-v4) activation path |
69. | `IsCapped is {0\|1}` | Whether enumeration is restricted to тЙдv2.0.50727 |
70. | `SkuCheckFlags are {value}` | SKU compatibility check mode |
71. | `ShouldEmulateExeLaunch is {0\|1}` | Whether to use EXE launch policies |
72. | `LegacyBindRequired is {0\|1}` | Whether legacy binding is strictly required |
73. | `Installed Runtime: vX.Y.Z. VERSION_ARCHITECTURE: N` | A runtime version found installed on the machine |
74. 
75. ### Config File Processing
76. 
77. | Message | Meaning |
78. |---------|---------|
79. | `Parsing config file: {path}` | Looking for an application config file |
80. | `Config File (Open). Result:00000000` | Config file found and opened successfully |
81. | `Config File (Open). Result:80070002` | Config file **not found** (ERROR_FILE_NOT_FOUND) |
82. | `Config File (Read). Result:00000000` | Config file read successfully |
83. | `Found config file: {path}` | Config file successfully parsed |
84. | `UseLegacyV2RuntimeActivationPolicy is set to {0\|1}` | Value of `<startup useLegacyV2RuntimeActivationPolicy="true\|false">` |
85. | `Config file includes SupportedRuntime entry. Version: {ver}, SKU: {sku}` | A `<supportedRuntime>` element from the config |
86. | `Found a supportedRuntime tag in the config file` | At least one `<supportedRuntime>` was present |
87. 
88. ### Runtime Selection Outcomes
89. 
90. | Message | Meaning |
91. |---------|---------|
92. | `Using supportedRuntime: vX.Y.Z` | Shim selected this version from config's `<supportedRuntime>` list |
93. | `{exe} was built with version: vX.Y.Z` | PE header version from a managed binary |
94. | `FindLatestVersion is returning the following version: vX.Y.Z Input VERSION_ARCHITECTURE: N, V2.0 Capped: {0\|1}` | Result of policy-based latest-version search |
95. | `Default version of the runtime on the machine: vX.Y.Z` | Resolved default version |
96. | `Default version of the runtime on the machine: (null)` | **No runtime found** тАФ resolution failed |
97. | `Decided on runtime: vX.Y.Z` | **Final decision** тАФ this version will be loaded |
98. | `Runtime has been loaded. Version: vX.Y.Z` | CLR successfully loaded into the process |
99. | `V2.0 Capping is preventing consideration of a newer runtime` | A v4+ runtime was skipped because capping is active |
100. 
101. ### Errors and FOD
102. 
103. | Message | Meaning |
104. |---------|---------|
105. | `ERROR: Unable to find a version of the runtime to use.` | Version resolution failed completely |
106. | `ERROR: Version vX.Y.Z is not present on the machine.` | A specific requested version is not installed |

 ## Enabling Logging
 
 Set one of:
 - **Environment variable:** `COMPLUS_CLRLoadLogDir=C:\path\to\logdir`
 - **Registry:** `HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\.NETFramework\CLRLoadLogDir` (REG_SZ)
 - On 64-bit, also `HKLM\SOFTWARE\Wow6432Node\Microsoft\.NETFramework\CLRLoadLogDir` for 32-bit processes
 
 > тЪая╕П **The log directory must already exist.** The shim will not create it. If it doesn't exist, no logs will be written and there will be no error or indication of failure.
 
 Logs are written by mscoreei.dll (the shim implementation DLL loaded by mscoree.dll). They are written while the process runs and **cannot be opened until the process exits**.
 
 ## File Naming
 
 ```
 {ProcessName}.CLRLoad{NN}.log
 ```
 
 - `{ProcessName}` = the EXE name (e.g., `mt.exe`, `csc.exe`, `MSBuild.exe`)
 - `{NN}` = sequence number 00тАУ99 (increments if the previous file exists, one per process instance)
 - Multiple log files for the same EXE indicate multiple process invocations
 
 ## Line Format
 
 Every line in the log follows this format:
 
 ```
 ThreadID,TickCount.Milliseconds,Message
 ```
 
 - **ThreadID**: OS thread ID (decimal) that produced the log entry
 - **TickCount.Milliseconds**: Time in `GetTickCount()` units тАФ seconds since system boot, with millisecond precision. Useful for ordering events and measuring elapsed time within and across logs.
 - **Message**: The log message (see below)
 
 ### Header Lines
 
 The first three lines of every log are:
 
 ```
 {tid},{tick},CLR Loading log for {full_path_to_exe}
 {tid},{tick},Log started at {time} on {date}
 {tid},{tick},-----------------------------------
 ```
 
 The process path in the first line identifies exactly which binary triggered CLR activation.

 # CLR Activation Log Format Reference
 
 ## Enabling Logging
 
 Set one of:
 - **Environment variable:** `COMPLUS_CLRLoadLogDir=C:\path\to\logdir`
 - **Registry:** `HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\.NETFramework\CLRLoadLogDir` (REG_SZ)
 - On 64-bit, also `HKLM\SOFTWARE\Wow6432Node\Microsoft\.NETFramework\CLRLoadLogDir` for 32-bit processes
 
 > тЪая╕П **The log directory must already exist.** The shim will not create it. If it doesn't exist, no logs will be written and there will be no error or indication of failure.
 
 Logs are written by mscoreei.dll (the shim implementation DLL loaded by mscoree.dll). They are written while the process runs and **cannot be opened until the process exits**.
 
 ## File Naming
 
 ```
 {ProcessName}.CLRLoad{NN}.log
 ```
 
 - `{ProcessName}` = the EXE name (e.g., `mt.exe`, `csc.exe`, `MSBuild.exe`)
 - `{NN}` = sequence number 00тАУ99 (increments if the previous file exists, one per process instance)
 - Multiple log files for the same EXE indicate multiple process invocations
 
 ## Line Format
 
 Every line in the log follows this format:
 
 ```
 ThreadID,TickCount.Milliseconds,Message
 ```
 
 - **ThreadID**: OS thread ID (decimal) that produced the log entry
 - **TickCount.Milliseconds**: Time in `GetTickCount()` units тАФ seconds since system boot, with millisecond precision. Useful for ordering events and measuring elapsed time within and across logs.
 - **Message**: The log message (see below)
 
 ### Header Lines
 
 The first three lines of every log are:
 
 ```
 {tid},{tick},CLR Loading log for {full_path_to_exe}
 {tid},{tick},Log started at {time} on {date}
 {tid},{tick},-----------------------------------
 ```
 
 The process path in the first line identifies exactly which binary triggered CLR activation.

data.content:
The logs are nearly identical тАФ the **only difference** is `SEM_FAILCRITICALERRORS`:

| Launch method | `SEM_FAILCRITICALERRORS` | Result |
|---|---|---|
| Script | **32769** (0x8001) | FOD **suppressed** тАФ "Could have launched..." |
| cmd.exe | **0** | FOD **launched** тАФ dialog shown |

Both logs fail the same way: `DllGetClassObject` for CorSymWriter (`{E5CB7A31-...}`) тЖТ legacy bind with `IsCapped: 1` тЖТ no `mt.exe.config` found тЖТ only v4.0 installed тЖТ capped enumeration sees nothing тЖТ `(null)` тЖТ ERROR.

The activation failure is **always happening** in both cases. The difference is purely whether the FOD dialog is displayed:

- **Your script** (or its parent process) calls `SetErrorMode` with `SEM_FAILCRITICALERRORS` (0x8001 = `SEM_FAILCRITICALERRORS | SEM_NOGPFAULTERRORBOX`). Child processes inherit this, so mt.exe silently swallows the failure.
- **cmd.exe** has error mode 0 by default, so mt.exe shows the FOD dialog.

**To fix the cmd.exe case**, create `C:\Tools\x86\mt.exe.config`:

```xml
<?xml version="1.0"?>
<configuration>
  <startup useLegacyV2RuntimeActivationPolicy="true">
    <supportedRuntime version="v4.0"/>
  </startup>
</configuration>
```

This lifts the Whidbey cap so the legacy COM activation can find and use v4.0, eliminating the failure (and the FOD dialog) regardless of error mode.

**mt-from-script.CLRLoad00.txt** (line 20):
```
SEM_FAILCRITICALERRORS is set to 32769
```
Result (line 37): `Could have launched feature-on-demand installation if was not opted out.`

**mt-from-cmdline.CLRLoad00.txt** (line 20):
```

```
Result (line 37): `Launching feature-on-demand installation.`

So it comes down to `SEM_FAILCRITICALERRORS`. The script sets it to 32769 (which combines the flag with SEM_NOGPFAULTERRORBOX), suppressing the FOD dialog, while the direct cmd.exe launch has it at 0, allowing the dialog to appear. The script version is the working one that avoids the dialog, whereas the command-line version triggers it.
```
